item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Slated for revision, see Addenda — O5-2

Addendum: On November 2nd, 2016, all subjects affected by SCP-1328 began manifesting a delusion that they were citizens of the Russian Federation. Amnestic treatments have been unable to alter this belief. In addition, most affected subjects appear to be displaying signs of cognitive dissonance, with erratic and unpredictable behaviors resulting.

(I feel that these additions do not add anything to the story, in fact they break with the sense of the story, they are only there to connect with 001 and that's it.)

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image used in this article comes from Wikimedia Commons under Public Domain, and thus is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not much more can be said in regards to critique of this one that hasn't been stated above. As with many others the 'Chasm opens up' bit is what pushed me to a -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was willing to novote this and move on, but the Serapis edit pushed me to downvote. I don't think the Serapis edits are very good, and this is one of the worst. -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Hmm, wonder why this is so low in the votes department?" I asked myself when I started reading this article. Then a chasm opened up and started swallowing dudes, and I was all like "Ah, theeerrrreee it is."

Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is great, but it's poorly written, and I mean poorly written. I've seen many other scips that were not as horribly written as this one being downvoted into oblivion, so its current +17 (after my downvote) strikes me as surprising. Some people above have stated that the concept has similarities with other scips, I agree but also think that it can stand easily on its own, if only someone corrected the horrible writing (It's really baaaaaaaaaaaad) And some other minor problems (lenght, redundancies, the way it's not very clear about what's modern and what's old, and maybe expanding it a little in the right directions). This scip has a lot of potential. It just needs a capable writer that fixes it.

Downvoted

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mmm. Neh. Meh? Yllllmmmmnnn hmm. … -1

item.title: Re: Silent-hill-esque chasm?
item.content:encoded: Although I am necroposting I still feel moved to point out that language is very rude.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this idea, but the way it's executed is awkward at times and ultimately anticlimatic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Negative vote, for many of the reasons cited above (foremost the concept being sort of "blah") but what really crystallized my downvote was the "chasm opens up beneath the traveler and teleports them to SCP-1328" aspect. It strikes me as the sort of "vulgar display of power" characteristic of the more unfocused/uninteresting SCPs.
The teleporting phenomenon, the means by which the chasm is opened, the connection between the chasm and the geographic region etc are not adiquately explored enough to be interesting from a "brain teaser" perspective, but too straightforward for the "mysterious" aspect to be creepy. I also consider the teleportation to be narratively superfluous, as the core of the SCP would be just as effective if individuals made their trek via conventional means and arrived within conventional timescales expected for such a journey.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Neutral vote.

Odd tone, but I sort of like the concept from one perspective: is Israel/Palestine a lesser form of this phenomenon that has managed to balloon way out of containment?

Other people have great ideas to clean it up: take out the chasms, make it more interesting, add a hook. (Possibly the Palestine connection)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Occasionally, maps, books, and other cartographic references will begin listing SCP-1328 as being part of another country, adding an "extra state" to their maps.

Affected subjects may begin identifying themselves as residents of SCP-1328, and attempt to navigate to it overland.

Subjects from different nations may encounter each other, which can cause confusion and anger due to SCP-1328's effect.

Wait, so these altered maps will appear in locations outside the United States? Then the eastern region of Tennessee isn't the SCP, it's just that whatever is turning the maps paranormal has an interest in that region.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A couple of things that jumped out at me:

-"…to be issued amnestics." You use that verb twice, and surely you mean "dosed" or "treated" or something else that means actually giving them the drug as opposed to just handing out the pills.

-Like Ostberg said above, is it a phenomenon or a region? It seems to me that the anomaly here is actually the phenomenon, the place itself is normal for anyone not exposed to erroneous maps. If you are married to the idea of it being both, might I suggest "a region (insert description here) and associated psychological/cartographical anomalies."

-"extra state" This seems like one of those "let's use qoutes to get away with non-technical language" situations. Although I'll admit I don't have enough cartography and/or geopolitical background to say what the technical wording should be, if any. And what about countries that don't have states? If it appears on a map of Canada, shouldn't it show as an "extra province", for example?

-I am not a fan of the chasm. But this is strictly a matter of personal taste. Reminds me too much of the opening for the old "Land of the Lost" tv show.
-Always overland? What if they are on an island so tiny that it takes less than 2 hours to traverse?

-"Originally believed to be a transportation anomaly…" I think you need to stick a "strictly" or "limited" in their. Because as it stands, this phrase implies that we've learned better and that 1328 has nothing to do with transportation. That's obviously not the case.

-"…returned home" You use this phrase several times in the logs, and it just isn't clinical at all. It's also not always accurate to the situation described. What about the El Salvodorian's relatives? They resided in Franklin, and apparently had for some time. Did they get sent back to El Salvodor, or to their houses in Tennessee?

-Regarding the Prussian, it's confusing to have him at the end of the list. The rest appear to be at least relatively modern cases, but the Prussian is from a much earlier period. If all the cases are from the ASCI archives it should say so explicitly at the start of the logs, otherwise the earliest case should be first, not last.

-The Somalian. Why is this complicated? The region is in turmoil, and I get that repatriation would present a sensitive problem for, say, the US government. But if anyone knows how to make problems disappear, it's The Foundation. If all else fails, I'm sure there's a spare orange jumpsuit and an empty bunk in the D-class dorms.

-Michael Sen is a leader in the "Regular Alliance Troupe". But the homes belonged to the "Red Actors Troupe." Is the switch on purpose, am I missing a reference?

All in all, I like it, but I'd like it more if there was more exploration of the implications. The extreme balkanization and bizarre alliances could be juicy stuff indeed. You hint at it in the last addendum, but I really want to know how the Canadian-Peruvian faction is faring against the more militaristic Haitian-Tibetan-Sioux collective.

item.title: Silent-hill-esque chasm?
item.content:encoded: It's interesting, even cool, but ya fuckin' lost me when dudes started just walking into chasms and arriving in "Franklin". Apart from being retarded magical crap it's way less interesting than if they just started to travel and inexplicably arrived two hours later through time/ physics warping.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It feels like it doesn't go far enough, Scantron (& others) is (are) right that it doesn't explore enough of the interesting implications of the idea, and my eyes seriously glazed over reading most of it, which is no bueno. Detail work still has to be interesting of its own merits, and interesting for the reader as opposed to interesting to the Foundation (if the latter, it can simply be abbreviated, a technique used by many shorter articles).

…but I still loved the concept enough to upvote :P I can't help it, this shit is like candy to me. You should still work on it though. Because it needs it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, and I don't think it needs that much work. Something I find strange, though is that it says

SCP-1328 is a phenomenon affecting the eastern portion of the state of Tennessee in the U.S.

and then it says

Occasionally, maps, books, and other cartographic references will begin listing SCP-1328 as being part of another country…

So, is it a place, or a phenomenon, or am I just having a loose grasp on my concepts today? Still up-voting, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It doesn't explore any interesting implications of the idea, and the compulsion is silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, It needs work though, like Breggren said

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Agreed. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, seems too much like Samothrace to me.
Neutral vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it was pretty interesting, but has too many proofreading errors for an upvote. Also, I'm not really sure about any of the addenda after the first, especially the one about the Somalian.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, but it needs work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hesse was an independent state until 1871.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not necessarily. The term "Hessian" refers to people from Hesse, which, in 1866, was an independent country. "Briton" is a bit more of an odd phrase (I feel like "Englishman", "Scotsman", "Welshman", or "Irishman" might have been more appropriate, depending on where the particular person came from). Then again, I'm really not sure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm equally lukewarm on this. As others have stated, it's fairly similar to a number of other entries, without bringing anything substantively new to the concept. A number of errors in tone and grammar hurt the flow-though that's obviously a minor problem and could be easily fixed-and the addenda aren't strong enough to carry the weight this piece needs carried. Their formatting is also a bit choppy, which further hurts any building of narrative momentum.

Along the lines of praise, though, the first addendum carries the parachronistic voice with a nice subtlety, and the Foundation's confusion on what to do with their Somalian houseguest holds promise for me on differentiating 1328 from Samothrace et al. Others might disagree with me on this, but I think developing that, and focusing on how the Foundation operates and interacts with others within the broader political context, would be an excellent start; for me Samothrace is mostly about how we perceive the political situation, and while this certainly has those elements, it also has the potential to discuss the more practical ramifications of foreign policy as filtered through the Illuminati.

No vote, for the moment.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: bland

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I seem to be in the same wagon as the others. This is a very minimalist SCP, without any hooks to catch the reader. The attempt at humor at the end makes the SCP feel bland and forced, as if the punchline were thought up first and then the joke was written to give it context.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It needs some proofreading, and while I like the idea, I think you could do more with it. No vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Complete meh.
Reminds me of Samothrace and of that Jugoslavian flag which made people try to claim it, except being less interesting than either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I give this a resounding "I am uninterested."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this a lot, except for this line.

"When first discovered, over twenty Hessians, a Briton, and a Cherokee Indian were found living in it."

That seems…a bit anachronistic to me? 1866 was Civil War era, that's coming off to me as Revolutionary War or just after.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He's not from modern times. The guy was contained by the ASCI in 1866, then given to local law people because they didn't have anything to do with him and it wasn't worth it to send him back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Discovered during initial containment, subject appeared to be an officer in the Prussian army. Believed to be instance by the ASCI, he was given to local law enforcement."

I have a problem with this. "Prussian army" implicates he's from XIX century Germany - a modern military man would be referred to as a "German officer". So why does the Foundation release him to local law enforcement without applying amnesiacs? They'll quickly find out that he's not from our time period… Also a number of mistakes like "peoples" or "these nation's".

But the overall idea is interesting and has a lot of potential. Upvoting for this reason.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not bad, but not catching me. I like that it was originally discovered/"contained" by the ASCI, but the reports from them don't really read like documents from that time period. No vote for now while I ponder on it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This idea makes me giggle.

+1

Save Franklin!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
An interesting if not particularly novel idea, but I'm just not feeling enough out of it to upvote.

Also, lots of minor errors like "nations" instead of "nation's" and misplaced punctuation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is thoroughly meh to me. Certainly not poorly written, but the effect wasn't really clear in the description and when it became apparent in the addenda, it wasn't very interesting.
